---
title: "Assignment 1"
author: "Simon Ganter, Josia John, Tudor Luchiean, Leif Maurer"
format: html
execute:
  cache: false 
---

\newpage

# Task 1: Geometrically Weighted Terms in ERGMs

## (1) Change statistic of gwodegree

### i.

Let $x'$ be the network obtained by adding a directed edge to a sender node that currently has out-degree $k\ge 1$ in $x$. Now:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_\text{GWD}(x,\alpha) &= e^\alpha \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(1 - \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^i\right) \cdot D_i^\text{out}(x)\\
\\
s_\text{GWD}(x',\alpha) &= e^\alpha \cdot \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(1 - \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^i\right) \cdot D_i^\text{out}(x) - \left(1 - \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^k\right) + \left(1 - \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^{k+1}\right)\right)\\
\\
\delta_\text{GWD}(k,\alpha) &= s_\text{GWD}(x',\alpha) - s_\text{GWD}(x,\alpha)\\
&= e^\alpha \cdot \left(- \left(1 - \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^k\right) + \left(1 - \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^{k+1}\right)\right)\\
&= e^\alpha \cdot \left(\left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^k - \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^{k+1}\right)\\
&= e^a \cdot \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^k \cdot \left(1 - \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)\right)\\
&= e^a \cdot \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^k \cdot e^{-\alpha}\\
&= \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)^k\\
\end{aligned}
$$

For $k=0$ we get $\delta_\text{GWD}(0,\alpha) = e^\alpha \cdot \left(1 - \left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)\right) = e^\alpha\cdot e^{-\alpha} = 1$. Note that this agrees with plugging in $k=0$ in the result above.

### ii.

```{r}
delta <- function(k, alpha) {
    return((1-exp(-alpha))^k)
}


f1 <- as.matrix(read.csv('../data/f1.csv'))
outdegrees <- rowSums(f1)
min <- min(outdegrees)
max <- max(outdegrees)

makeplot <- function(alphas) {
    x <- min:max
    ys <- lapply(alphas, function(a) delta(x, a))
    cols <- rainbow(length(alphas))

    plot(
    x, ys[[1]],
    type = "o",
    col = cols[1],
    ylim = range(unlist(ys)),
    xlab = "x",
    ylab = "delta(x, alpha)"
    )

    for (i in seq_along(alphas)) {
    lines(x, ys[[i]], type = "o", col = cols[i])
    }

    legend(
    "topright",
        legend = paste("alpha =", round(alphas, 3)),
        col = cols,
        lty = 1,
        pch = 1,
        cex = 0.8
    )
}

makeplot(c(-1))
makeplot(c(-0.5))
makeplot(c(0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, log(2), 1, 2, 3))
```

### iii.

The first observation is that $\alpha<0$ usually doesn't make sense because it distinguishes between odd and even outdegree very strongly.

Next, if $\alpha$ is close to zero, then only the first edge contributes significantly to the `gwodegree` statistic. 

[TODO]

### iv.

Large. [TODO]

## (2) 

## (3)
